India.title: Election Announcement
India.beginner: Like setting the date and rules for a big nationwide festival.
India.advanced: The Election Commission of India (ECI) announces the schedule, instantly enforcing the Model Code of Conduct (MCC) to ensure fair play.

India.title: Filing Nominations
India.beginner: Players signing up to join the game.
India.advanced: Candidates submit their nomination papers and affidavits detailing their assets, liabilities, and criminal records.

India.title: Campaigning
India.beginner: Candidates trying to convince you why they are the best choice.
India.advanced: Parties release manifestos, hold rallies, and use media. Campaigning must legally stop 48 hours before polling begins.

India.title: Voting Day
India.beginner: Everyone goes to the polling booth to press a button for their favorite.
India.advanced: Citizens use Electronic Voting Machines (EVMs) with VVPAT (Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail) for transparency.

India.title: Counting & Results
India.beginner: Adding up all the points to see who won.
India.advanced: EVMs are securely transported and counted under heavy security. The party with at least 272 out of 543 Lok Sabha seats forms the government.

India.title: Government Formation
India.beginner: The winning team's captain becomes the Prime Minister.
India.advanced: The President invites the leader of the majority party or coalition to take the oath of office as Prime Minister.

USA.title: Primaries & Caucuses
USA.beginner: Teams picking their best player for the final match.
USA.advanced: States hold primary elections or caucuses to award delegates to presidential candidates for the national conventions.

USA.title: National Conventions
USA.beginner: A big party where the team officially announces their player.
USA.advanced: Parties officially nominate their presidential and vice-presidential candidates based on delegate votes.

USA.title: General Campaigning
USA.beginner: The two main players travel everywhere asking for your vote.
USA.advanced: Candidates focus heavily on 'Swing States' where the vote could go either way, participating in televised debates.

USA.title: Election Day
USA.beginner: People go to vote, but they are actually voting for 'electors'.
USA.advanced: Voters cast ballots for a slate of electors pledged to a candidate, initiating the Electoral College process.

USA.title: Electoral College Vote
USA.beginner: The points from each state are officially counted.
USA.advanced: The 538 electors meet in their respective states to officially cast their votes for President and Vice President. 270 votes are needed to win.

USA.title: Inauguration
USA.beginner: The winner officially starts their new job.
USA.advanced: The President-elect takes the oath of office at the U.S. Capitol, officially beginning their four-year term.

UK.title: Dissolution of Parliament
UK.beginner: Everyone loses their job as an MP and has to reapply.
UK.advanced: The Monarch dissolves Parliament, usually at the Prime Minister's request, vacating all 650 seats in the House of Commons.

UK.title: The 'Purdah' Period
UK.beginner: The government isn't allowed to make big news to stay fair.
UK.advanced: Strict rules prevent civil servants and local government from making announcements that could influence the election outcome.

UK.title: Nominations
UK.beginner: Candidates officially enter the race in their local area.
UK.advanced: Candidates must submit nomination papers with signatures from 10 registered electors in their constituency and a £500 deposit.

UK.title: Campaigning
UK.beginner: Politicians knock on doors and debate on TV.
UK.advanced: Focused on local constituencies and national manifestos. TV debates are common, though not legally mandated.

UK.title: Polling Day
UK.beginner: People vote using a simple paper ballot.
UK.advanced: Voters use the First-Past-The-Post (FPTP) system, marking an 'X' next to their preferred local candidate.

UK.title: Results & Government
UK.beginner: The winner goes to see the King to become Prime Minister.
UK.advanced: If a party wins over 325 seats, their leader is invited by the Monarch to form a government. Otherwise, a 'hung parliament' results in coalition talks.

Australia.title: Issuing the Writs
Australia.beginner: The official starting gun for the election race.
Australia.advanced: The Governor-General issues formal documents (writs) commanding the Electoral Commissioner to hold an election.

Australia.title: Close of Rolls
Australia.beginner: The final deadline to register so you can vote.
Australia.advanced: The electoral roll is frozen. Voting in Australia is compulsory; failing to register or vote results in a fine.

Australia.title: Nominations
Australia.beginner: Candidates put their names on the ballot paper.
Australia.advanced: Candidates declare their intention to run for either the House of Representatives or the Senate, paying a deposit.

Australia.title: Campaigning & Pre-polling
Australia.beginner: People can actually vote early if they are busy on election day.
Australia.advanced: Early voting centers open for those unable to vote on polling day. Mobile polling visits remote areas and hospitals.

Australia.title: Election Day
Australia.beginner: Everyone votes and eats a 'Democracy Sausage'.
Australia.advanced: Voters use Preferential Voting for the House (ranking candidates 1, 2, 3...) and Proportional Representation for the Senate.

Australia.title: Results & Declaration
Australia.beginner: Counting the ranked votes to find the most popular winner.
Australia.advanced: The complex preferential counting can take time. The party commanding a majority (76 seats) in the House forms the government.

Germany.title: Election Date Set
Germany.beginner: The President picks a Sunday for the big vote.
Germany.advanced: The Federal President sets the date for the Bundestag election, which must be a Sunday or public holiday.

Germany.title: Candidate Nomination
Germany.beginner: Parties make lists of who they want in power.
Germany.advanced: Parties submit their state lists (Landeslisten) and direct candidates for the 299 constituencies.

Germany.title: Campaigning
Germany.beginner: Posters go up everywhere and parties share their plans.
Germany.advanced: State-funded parties focus heavily on policy platforms. TV debates between the main chancellor candidates are a highlight.

Germany.title: Election Day
Germany.beginner: You get two votes: one for a person, one for a party.
Germany.advanced: Voters cast a 'First Vote' for a local constituency representative (FPTP) and a 'Second Vote' for a party list (Proportional Representation).

Germany.title: Allocating Seats
Germany.beginner: Math is used to make sure the parliament matches the party votes exactly.
Germany.advanced: The Second Vote determines the total percentage of seats a party gets. 'Overhang' and 'Leveling' mandates are added to ensure pure proportionality.

Germany.title: Electing the Chancellor
Germany.beginner: Parties team up to get over 50% and pick the leader.
Germany.advanced: Because absolute majorities are rare, parties form coalitions. The newly formed Bundestag then officially elects the Chancellor via a secret ballot.
